name: Delta-V Budget Framework
summary: A systematic method for mapping the total velocity change required for a complete space mission. Every manoeuvre — launch, orbit transfer, landing, return — has a delta-v cost, and the budget must balance against propulsion capability.
whenToUseIt: Use this framework at the start of any mission design. Map every manoeuvre from launch to mission end, sum the delta-v requirements, add margins, and then size your vehicle around the total budget.
steps: Define mission objectives (destination orbit, landing site, return requirements)
Map every propulsive manoeuvre in the mission sequence
Assign delta-v cost to each manoeuvre (from astrodynamics calculations or reference tables)
Add margins for gravity losses, drag losses, navigation errors, and contingency
Use the Tsiolkovsky equation to size propellant, stages, and vehicle
Iterate to optimise — can you reduce delta-v with gravity assists, aerobraking, or better trajectories?
example: Mars mission delta-v budget: Earth launch to LEO (9.4 km/s) + LEO to Mars transfer (3.6 km/s) + Mars orbit insertion (1.0 km/s) + Mars landing (1.5 km/s) + Mars ascent (4.1 km/s) + Mars to Earth transfer (2.5 km/s) + Earth orbit insertion (1.0 km/s). Total: ~23.1 km/s. This is why a Mars return mission requires in-situ propellant production — no rocket can carry fuel for 23 km/s of delta-v from Earth.
